mnemonics.title: Dynamic Stack Resizing
mnemonics.content: When FULL, DOUBLE your space; When 1/4 FULL, HALVE your space. This prevents thrashing while conserving memory.

mnemonics.title: Amortized Cost Proof
mnemonics.content: Aggregate = Sum all costs, divide by n. For doubling: Total < 3n, so amortized < 3 = O(1). Simple!

mnemonics.title: Stack Operations
mnemonics.content: PUSH adds to top (increment first or store then increment depending on convention), POP removes from top (remove then decrement), PEEK views top without removing.

acronyms.usage: The fundamental property of stack data structure - the last element pushed is the first element popped.
Property of queues, opposite of LIFO stacks.
Stack is defined by its operations (push, pop, peek) without specifying implementation details.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a rubber band stretching: initially small, when it hits the limit it doubles in size. The old rubber band (data) is transferred to the new, bigger one.
Imagine paying $3 for every push. $1 is for this push, $2 is 'saved' to pay for future copying. When doubling happens, you use all saved money to pay for copying.
A balloon that only deflates when it's less than 1/4 full - too much deflating wastes energy (time), too little wastes air (memory).

analogies.analogy: Think of a hotel with an expandable number of rooms. When full, they build a new hotel twice as big, move all guests (data) to new hotel, then demolish old one. Guests don't notice the move (amortized).
Like a stack of plates: you add plates to the top (push) and remove from the top (pop). The 'top' variable tracks which plate is on top.
Houses on a street (array) vs houses scattered around (linked list). Delivery is faster on a street because all addresses are close together.
